title: Combat Structure and Initiative
description: Core framework for organizing and running combat encounters

combat_overview.description: A typical combat encounter is a clash between two sides: a flurry of weapon swings, feints, parries, footwork, and spellcasting
combat_overview.organization: The game organizes combat into a cycle of rounds and turns
combat_overview.time_scale: A round represents about 6 seconds in the game world
combat_overview.turn_structure: During a round, each participant in a battle takes a turn
combat_overview.continuation: Once everyone has taken a turn, the fight continues to the next round if neither side is defeated

combat_steps.title: Establish Positions
combat_steps.description: The Dungeon Master determines where all the characters and monsters are located. Given the adventurers' marching order or their stated positions in the room or other location, the DM figures out where the adversaries are—how far away and in what direction.

combat_steps.title: Roll Initiative
combat_steps.description: Everyone involved in the combat encounter rolls Initiative, determining the order of combatants' turns.

combat_steps.title: Take Turns
combat_steps.description: Each participant in the battle takes a turn in Initiative order. When everyone involved in the combat has had a turn, the round ends. Repeat this step until the fighting stops.

initiative.purpose: Initiative determines the order of turns during combat
initiative.mechanic: When combat starts, every participant rolls Initiative; they make a Dexterity check that determines their place in the Initiative order
initiative.dm_rolling: The DM rolls for monsters. For a group of identical creatures, the DM makes a single roll, so each member of the group has the same Initiative

surprise.description: If a combatant is surprised by combat starting, that combatant has Disadvantage on their Initiative roll
surprise.example: If an ambusher starts combat while hidden from a foe who is unaware that combat is starting, that foe is surprised

initiative_order.determination: A combatant's check total is called their Initiative count, or Initiative for short. The DM ranks the combatants, from highest to lowest Initiative
initiative_order.consistency: This is the order in which they act during each round. The Initiative order remains the same from round to round

ties.monster_ties: If a tie occurs, the DM decides the order among tied monsters
ties.player_ties: The players decide the order among tied characters
ties.mixed_ties: The DM decides the order if the tie is between a monster and a player character

your_turn.basic_actions: On your turn, you can move a distance up to your Speed and take one action. You decide whether to move first or take your action first
your_turn.action_reference: The main actions you can take are listed in 'Actions' earlier in this chapter. A character's features and a monster's stat block also provide action options

communicating.brief_communication: You can communicate however you are able—through brief utterances and gestures—as you take your turn. Doing so uses neither your action nor your move
communicating.extended_communication: Extended communication, such as a detailed explanation of something or an attempt to persuade a foe, requires an action. The Influence action is the main way you try to influence a monster

object_interaction.free_interaction: You can interact with one object or feature of the environment for free, during either your move or action. For example, you could open a door during your move as you stride toward a foe
object_interaction.additional_interactions: If you want to interact with a second object, you need to take the Utilize action. Some magic items and other special objects always require an action to use, as stated in their descriptions
object_interaction.dm_discretion: The DM might require you to use an action for any of these activities when it needs special care or when it presents an unusual obstacle. For instance, the DM might require you to take the Utilize action to open a stuck door or turn a crank to lower a drawbridge

doing_nothing.option: You can forgo moving, taking an action, or doing anything at all on your turn
doing_nothing.suggestions: If you can't decide what to do, consider taking the defensive Dodge action or the Ready action to delay acting

ending_combat.defeat_conditions: Combat ends when one side or the other is defeated, which can mean the creatures are killed or knocked out or have surrendered or fled
ending_combat.mutual_agreement: Combat can also end when both sides agree to end it

phases: Each combatant takes their turn in order
Round ends when all have acted
New round begins with same initiative order
turn_components: Any number of Reactions (on other turns)
